content.text: A language model never reads your text as characters. Before any inference begins, every byte of input — your prompt, the conversation history, system instructions, and any injected tool outputs — is converted into a sequence of integers called tokens. The fixed-length array that can hold those integers is the
. Understanding both concepts is foundational to building reliable agentic pipelines, estimating costs, and debugging the subtle failures that emerge when a model's memory runs out.

is the smallest discrete unit a language model processes. In modern transformer models the vocabulary is typically between 32 000 and 128 000 unique token IDs. Each ID maps to a short string of one or a few characters — rarely a single character, rarely a full English word. The tokenizer is trained jointly with the model, so the vocabulary is chosen to minimise the average number of tokens needed to represent the training corpus.

(also called the context length or sequence length) is the maximum number of token positions the model's attention mechanism can reference during a single forward pass. It is a hard architectural constant baked into the model at training time. If you provide more tokens than the context window allows, the API typically returns an error or silently truncates the input.
Both concepts are inseparable: the context window is measured in tokens, costs are billed in tokens, latency scales with token count, and quality degrades predictably as tokens approach the limit.
For an agentic system that runs multi-turn conversations, calls tools, injects documents, and accumulates reasoning traces, the context window is the system's

. Everything the agent knows about the current task must fit inside it. When the window fills up, the agent can no longer see its earlier decisions, the user's original request may be pushed out, and tool results from the first few steps become invisible.

Token cost matters too. API providers charge per token in and per token out. A naively built agent that echoes the entire conversation history on every turn pays an exponentially growing bill — O(n²) token cost for n turns — and hits the context limit correspondingly faster.

Finally, tokenization quirks explain many surprising model behaviours: why changing a variable name can shift a model's answer, why some languages cost five times more per word than English, and why a well-formatted prompt sometimes outperforms a rambling one even when they contain the same information.
Models with 128k+ windows can process entire codebases, long PDFs, or extended agentic trajectories without losing earlier context.
Instructions, few-shot examples, and schemas injected once at the start of the context remain accessible throughout the session without re-retrieval.
Because every input and output is counted in tokens, cost estimation is deterministic — you can budget accurately before deployment.
Byte-pair encoding handles any Unicode text, making the same model work across dozens of languages with no preprocessing change.
BPE keeps common words as single tokens while splitting rare words into recognisable parts, giving the model robust generalisation to novel vocabulary.
No matter how large, it will eventually fill. Long-running agents need active context management strategies.
Standard attention is O(n²) in sequence length. Very long contexts increase latency and compute cost super-linearly unless the architecture uses sparse or linear attention variants.
Token counts vary by language, formatting, and content type. Estimating tokens from character counts can be off by 3–5x for code-heavy or non-Latin text.

Empirical studies show that transformer models attend more reliably to tokens near the start and end of the context; information buried deep in the middle receives weaker attention even when it is technically inside the window.
Providers charge differently for cached vs uncached tokens. Cache hits only occur when the prefix matches exactly, so even minor prompt changes can invalidate the cache.

The tokenizer starts with a vocabulary of individual bytes (256 entries) and then repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols in the training corpus, adding the merged symbol as a new vocabulary entry. This process repeats until the vocabulary reaches the target size (e.g., 100 277 tokens for cl100k_base used by GPT-4).
The result is a vocabulary where common English words map to one or two tokens, common subwords are tokens (e.g.,
), and rare strings are decomposed into their byte components. The encoding is deterministic and prefix-free.

Given an input string, the tokenizer runs a greedy longest-match scan using the merge rules it learned during training. The output is a list of integer token IDs, one per token segment. These IDs are the actual inputs to the model's embedding layer.
Step 3 — The attention mechanism and context window

Inside the transformer, each token position generates a query, key, and value vector. The self-attention operation computes pairwise similarities between every query and every key, producing attention weights, and uses those weights to aggregate value vectors. The context window is the size of this pairwise comparison matrix. For a window of n tokens, the attention matrix is n × n, which explains the quadratic scaling.

import tiktoken

# cl100k_base is used by GPT-4 and text-embedding-ada-002
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

text = "Agentic AI systems use tool calling to interact with external APIs."
tokens = enc.encode(text)

print(f"Text  : {text!r}")
print(f"Tokens: {tokens}")
print(f"Count : {len(tokens)}")

# Decode each token to see the mapping
for tok_id in tokens:
    segment = enc.decode([tok_id])
    print(f"  {tok_id:>6}  {segment!r}")

The diagram below shows the full pipeline from raw text through tokenization to the context window layout used by an agentic system, along with a comparison of context window sizes across frontier models.
enters the tokenizer as a UTF-8 byte stream.
applies learned merge rules to produce token segments. The output is a list of human-readable strings.
maps each segment to its integer token ID from the vocabulary table.
converts each ID to a dense floating-point vector (e.g., 1536 dimensions for some models).
process the full sequence of embedding vectors with multi-head self-attention, bounded by the context window length.
for an agentic system typically has: system prompt, compressed conversation history, tool results, the new user turn, and a reserved output budget.
Understanding context windows and tokens is relevant in every situation where you interact with an LLM API, but it becomes especially important in these scenarios:
— each iteration appends tool inputs and outputs; you must track cumulative token usage to avoid overflow.
— if a document is larger than the context window, you must chunk and retrieve rather than stuffing the whole document in.
— adding more examples improves accuracy but burns context; you must balance the two.
— before deploying a pipeline, count expected tokens per call to model the monthly bill.
— different models have different tokenizers; token counts for the same text differ between GPT-4 and Claude.
— to get cache hits, the cached prefix must be both identical and long enough to amortise the cache write cost.
— billing happens on output tokens as they stream; knowing the max output budget helps cap runaway generation.
The following patterns cover the three most important implementation tasks: counting tokens before a call, tracking usage during a call, and managing context growth in an agentic loop.

import tiktoken
from anthropic import Anthropic

# ─── 1. Count tokens before sending ───────────────────────────────────────────
def count_tokens_openai(text: str, model: str = "gpt-4o") -> int:
    """Return the token count for a text string using tiktoken."""
    encoding_name = tiktoken.encoding_name_for_model(model)
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(encoding_name)
    return len(enc.encode(text))

sample = "Summarise the key risks in the attached contract."
print(f"Token count: {count_tokens_openai(sample)}")  # 9

# ─── 2. Track usage via the API response ──────────────────────────────────────
client = Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain byte-pair encoding in two sentences."}],
)

usage = response.usage
print(f"Input tokens  : {usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens : {usage.output_tokens}")
print(f"Total tokens  : {usage.input_tokens + usage.output_tokens}")

# ─── 3. Sliding-window context management ─────────────────────────────────────
MAX_CONTEXT = 100_000   # leave headroom below the model's 200k limit
MAX_RESPONSE = 4_096

def trim_history(messages: list[dict], system_tokens: int) -> list[dict]:
    """Drop the oldest turns until the total fits in the budget."""
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
    budget = MAX_CONTEXT - system_tokens - MAX_RESPONSE

    while messages:
        total = sum(len(enc.encode(m["content"])) for m in messages)
        if total <= budget:
            break
        # Drop the oldest user+assistant pair
        messages = messages[2:]
    return messages
Counting tokens for multiple content types

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

# English prose — roughly 1 token per 4 chars
prose = "The transformer architecture relies on self-attention mechanisms."
print(f"Prose   : {len(enc.encode(prose))} tokens  ({len(prose)} chars)")

# Python code — roughly 1 token per 3-4 chars
code = "def fibonacci(n: int) -> int:\n    return n if n < 2 else fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)"
print(f"Code    : {len(enc.encode(code))} tokens  ({len(code)} chars)")

# Japanese — roughly 1-2 tokens per character
japanese = "自然言語処理は人工知能の重要な分野です。"
print(f"Japanese: {len(enc.encode(japanese))} tokens  ({len(japanese)} chars)")

# Numbers — each digit is often its own token
numbers = "3.14159265358979323846"
print(f"Numbers : {len(enc.encode(numbers))} tokens  ({len(numbers)} chars)")
Estimating cost before calling the API

import tiktoken

# Approximate pricing as of mid-2025 (USD per million tokens)
PRICING = {
    "claude-sonnet-4-5": {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00},
    "claude-haiku-3-5":  {"input": 0.80, "output": 4.00},
    "gpt-4o":            {"input": 2.50, "output": 10.00},
}

def estimate_cost(prompt: str, expected_output_tokens: int, model: str) -> float:
    """Return estimated USD cost for a single API call."""
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
    input_tokens = len(enc.encode(prompt))
    rates = PRICING[model]
    input_cost  = input_tokens          * rates["input"]  / 1_000_000
    output_cost = expected_output_tokens * rates["output"] / 1_000_000
    return input_cost + output_cost

prompt = "Translate the following 500-word article into French: " + "word " * 500
cost = estimate_cost(prompt, expected_output_tokens=700, model="claude-sonnet-4-5")
print(f"Estimated cost: ${cost:.4f}")

Consider a customer support agent that handles product return requests. Each conversation includes a 400-token system prompt, the customer's order history (up to 3 000 tokens), and a multi-turn dialogue. After 20 turns, the conversation history alone can exceed 15 000 tokens. Without context management, the agent will:
Hit the context limit and receive an API error, crashing the session.
Lose visibility of the original order details when old turns are truncated.
Accumulate quadratic API costs — turn 20 costs 20x the per-token price of turn 1.
A production implementation addresses this with three mechanisms:
After every 10 turns, a secondary LLM call compresses the earliest turns into a 200-token summary that replaces them in the history.
The system prompt and the order history are always kept at the front of the context so the model never loses the core task definition.
Before every API call, the code asserts that input tokens + max_tokens <= context_window, raising an error early rather than receiving a cryptic API failure.

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()
CONTEXT_LIMIT = 200_000
MAX_OUTPUT    = 2_048

def summarise_turns(turns: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Compress a block of turns into a short summary."""
    combined = "\n".join(f"{m['role'].upper()}: {m['content']}" for m in turns)
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-3-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Summarise this conversation in under 200 words:\n{combined}"
        }],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

def agent_turn(system: str, history: list[dict], user_msg: str) -> str:
    """Run one agent turn with automatic context trimming."""
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_msg})

    # Compress if we're getting close to the limit
    # (use Anthropic's token-counting endpoint in production)
    total_chars = sum(len(m["content"]) for m in history)
    if total_chars > 80_000:          # rough heuristic: 80k chars ≈ 20k tokens
        old_turns  = history[:-4]     # keep the last 2 turns intact
        summary    = summarise_turns(old_turns)
        history    = [{"role": "assistant", "content": f"[Summary] {summary}"}] + history[-4:]

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=MAX_OUTPUT,
        system=system,
        messages=history,
    )
    reply = response.content[0].text
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
    return reply, history

The most common source of unexpected context overflows is estimating token count from character count. The ratio varies by content: English prose is roughly 4 characters per token, Python code is 3–4, and Mandarin Chinese can be 1–2 characters per token (each character is a separate token in many vocabularies). Always use the model's actual tokenizer for counting.
Ignoring output tokens in the budget
The context window covers both input and output tokens. If you set

and your input is already 124 000 tokens for a 128k-window model, the model may truncate its output or refuse the request. Always subtract the output budget from your input limit: input budget = context_window - max_output_tokens.
Sending the full conversation history on every turn
This pattern produces O(n²) token consumption. After 50 turns, turn 51 carries the entire text of turns 1–50. Use rolling summaries or a sliding window.
Assuming the same text has the same token count across models

GPT-4 uses cl100k_base; Claude uses its own tokenizer; Llama 3 uses a SentencePiece vocabulary. The same 1 000-word document may tokenize to 280 tokens in one model and 310 in another. Always count tokens using the specific model's tokenizer.
Confusing context window with model knowledge

The context window is the model's working memory for the current conversation. It has nothing to do with the model's training-time knowledge cutoff. A model with a 200k context window still only knows facts from before its training cutoff.
count characters and divide by 4 for production systems — use the tokenizer.
forget to reserve output tokens in your input budget.
append the full history every turn — implement sliding window or summarisation.
assume token counts are portable across different models.
Count tokens on realistic inputs before deploying. Use the provider's count API (e.g.,
Allocate explicit portions of the context window: e.g., 10% system prompt, 60% history, 20% tool results, 10% output reserve. Enforce these at runtime.
System tokens appear on every call. A 1 000-token system prompt on 10 000 calls costs 10M extra input tokens. Trim aggressively.
Use context caching for stable prefixes.
Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google all support prompt caching. A long stable prefix (system prompt + background docs) written once can be reused cheaply across thousands of calls.
Dropping turns silently corrupts the agent's memory. Summarise old turns before they leave the window.
Pin the most important tokens at the start and end.
Due to the "lost in the middle" effect, place critical instructions in the system prompt (start) or the last user message (end).
Tracking usage over time reveals trends: which agent nodes are token-heavy, which prompts are growing, and when a refactor is needed.

Attention is the dominant cost in transformer inference. The time complexity per forward pass is O(n²d) where n is the sequence length and d is the model dimension. Doubling the context length quadruples the attention compute cost while only increasing capacity linearly. This makes context management a scalability concern, not just a billing concern.

When the leading k tokens are identical across calls, providers can skip recomputing the KV cache for those positions. Anthropic charges a lower rate for cache reads and a modest premium for cache writes. The break-even is typically around 2–3 re-uses of a cached prefix.
Streaming returns tokens as they are generated, reducing perceived latency without changing the token count. It does not reduce cost.

Use a fast, cheap model (e.g., Haiku) for token counting, summarisation, and classification; reserve the large model for generation. This is the most impactful cost optimisation in multi-step pipelines.

Rather than embedding an entire document in the context, split it into overlapping chunks, embed each chunk, and retrieve only the most relevant chunks per query (RAG). This converts a O(document_length) context cost into a O(chunk_size) cost.
to the minimum needed for the task. Output tokens cost 3–5x more per token than input tokens on most providers.
(OpenAI) — the fastest Python tokenizer for GPT-family models. Supports cl100k_base and o200k_base vocabularies. Available on PyPI.
for exact Claude token counts before committing to a call.
(HuggingFace) — a Rust-backed Python library supporting BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece. Used for open-source models including Llama, Mistral, and Falcon.
— both include token-aware text splitters that split documents at token boundaries rather than character counts.
— observability platforms that capture and dashboard token usage across all API calls, enabling per-user and per-endpoint cost tracking.
— a browser-based tool at platform.openai.com/tokenizer that visualises token boundaries in any input text.
— a unified proxy that normalises token counting and cost estimation across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and other providers.
Several approaches exist for giving LLMs access to information that does not fit in the context window. Each involves a different trade-off between simplicity, latency, and accuracy.
Retrieve relevant chunks by embedding similarity
Compress old turns with a secondary LLM call
Store facts in a database; retrieve by ID or search
Re-use computed KV cache for stable prefix
Fixed system prompts, long docs used repeatedly

When tool outputs, retrieved documents, or user-supplied data are injected into the context, malicious content can attempt to override the system prompt or extract information from earlier turns. Mitigations include: wrapping injected content in clear delimiters, instructing the model to treat injected content as untrusted, and never including sensitive secrets in context that will be passed to tools.

An unhandled context overflow causes the API to return a 400 error mid-conversation, crashing the agent. Always implement a pre-flight check: count tokens before each call and handle the over-budget case explicitly — either by trimming history, raising a structured error, or triggering a summarisation step.

Everything in the context window is sent to the model provider. In regulated environments, avoid including PII, secrets, or confidential data in the context unless the provider's data processing agreement explicitly covers it. Consider tokenising or masking sensitive fields before injection and expanding them from a secure store post-generation.

Token IDs are discrete; even temperature=0 sampling can produce slightly different outputs across API versions if the model's logit distribution changes between updates. Do not rely on exact token-level output reproducibility for security-critical decisions.
Always validate and sanitise tool outputs before injecting them into the context.
Implement token budget assertions as a hard invariant, not a soft warning.
Audit which parts of the context contain PII before sending to external APIs.
Use structured output formats (JSON schema) to limit the model's ability to generate arbitrary content that could be injected downstream.
are the unit of LLM input and cost. English prose averages 4 characters per token; code and non-Latin scripts differ significantly.
is the model's hard working memory limit. Input + output tokens combined must not exceed it.
is the dominant tokenization algorithm for frontier models. It merges frequent byte pairs to build a vocabulary that balances coverage and efficiency.
consume context at O(n²) rate without management. Use sliding windows, summarisation, or RAG to keep consumption bounded.
at the start and end of context to avoid "lost in the middle" attention degradation.
Use the provider's tokenizer or counting API — never estimate from character count in production.
can dramatically reduce costs when a large stable prefix (system prompt + background docs) is reused across many calls.
than input tokens (3–5x on most providers). Set
Token counts are not portable between GPT-4, Claude, and open-source models.
sanitise injected content, assert token budgets, and avoid sending PII to external model APIs without explicit data processing agreements.

Think of the context window as a scroll of paper with a fixed physical length. You and the model can only read what is on the scroll right now — nothing more. You can write new content at the bottom, but when the scroll is full, you must either cut from the top (sliding window) or compress existing content into a shorter summary before adding more.

Tokens are the ink strokes on that scroll. Latin letters are cheap — each word costs 1–2 strokes. Kanji or code symbols can cost more strokes per logical unit. And crucially, every stroke you use for input is a stroke you cannot use for the model's reply.

The practical implication: treat every token as a scarce resource. Write tight system prompts, summarise aggressively, cache stable content, and pick the smallest model that can do the sub-task well. This mindset —
— separates agentic systems that scale to thousands of turns from those that collapse after twenty.
